text: Hello, everyone. This is a patient with a BMI of about forty-two who has bilateral recurrent inguinal hernias. As you can see, he has a ten by fifteen centimeter piece of iPOM mesh for umbilical ventral hernia repair in the past. He has two pieces of Progrip in the left groin as well as a plug and an unknown piece of mesh in the right groin. Both were from previous open hernia repairs. As you can see the left groin MPO was directly completely blown out and the right groin contains a scrotal hernia containing a large amount of small bowel. He was very symptomatic from this hernia and therefore I consented him for bilateral inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon reduces the small bowel from the scrotal hernia in the right groin, working towards exposing both myopectenial orifices (MPO) despite their weakened state.

The surgeon measures the surgical space as 36 centimeters wide and 18 centimeters tall, then cuts a large piece of heavyweight barred mesh to fit these dimensions. A 12-millimeter port is placed in the left lower quadrant to insert the mesh into the surgical field.

The surgeon places a stiff heavyweight mesh over the exposed pelvic area, ensuring it covers both myopectineal orifices and is tucked into the deep retropubic space. The mesh's corner in the right lower quadrant appears too long and will be trimmed later.

The surgeon secures the mesh using multiple 3-0 Vicryl sutures, with two sutures attached to the left-sided Cooper's ligament, two to the right-sided Cooper's ligament, and one to the midline pubis. Additional sutures are placed along the upper border of the mesh, avoiding placement in the triangle of pain and triangle of doom.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh, ensuring it tucks perfectly into the retropubic space between the bladder and the pubic bones. Excess mesh in the posterior lateral area is trimmed and removed. The remaining mesh provides excellent overlap posteriorly and laterally. Due to unsecured areas in the posterior lateral corners, the surgeon applies fibrin glue for additional security to prevent the mesh from lifting.

The surgeon reconstructs the peritoneal flap, which appears damaged from the takedown of a large scleral hernia. The redundant hernia sac is reduced and resected to prevent potential entanglement with the small bowel. The toe port fascial defect is closed using an O Vicryl suture.
Yeah, patient went home the same day, did not have any complications. Thank you for watching.
